Learning Objective 39D

Development of the Human Skull & Deformational Plagiocephaly

UNE COM · Embryology · Langman's Ch. 10 + Lecture Notes

The Big Picture

The skull has two functional divisions: the neurocranium (vault + base that protects the brain) and the viscerocranium (the facial skeleton). Each has a membranous component and a cartilaginous component with distinct embryologic origins.

Core Concept Flat bones of the cranial vault → intramembranous ossification (mesenchyme converts directly to bone).
Bones of the cranial base → endochondral ossification (mesenchyme → cartilage model → bone).

Three mesenchyme sources build the skull: neural crest cells (face, part of vault, prechordal base), paraxial mesoderm (parietal bones, chordal base from occipital sclerotomes), and lateral plate mesoderm (minor role).

Neurocranium

1. Membranous Neurocranium (Cranial Vault)

The flat bones — frontal, parietal, squamous temporal, squamous occipital — form via intramembranous ossification. Mesenchyme from neural crest (frontal bones) and paraxial mesoderm (parietal bones) invests the developing brain, and needle-like bone spicules radiate outward from primary ossification centers. After birth the vault grows by appositional deposition on the outer surface with simultaneous osteoclastic resorption from the inside.

Langman's Fig. 10.4 — Skeletal structures of the head and face. Neural crest (blue), paraxial mesoderm/somites (red), lateral plate mesoderm (yellow). From Langman's Medical Embryology, 15e.

2. Cartilaginous Neurocranium (Chondrocranium / Cranial Base)

The cranial base begins as separate cartilage plates that fuse and undergo endochondral ossification. A critical anatomical landmark divides the base into two embryologic domains:

The Dividing Line The rostral limit of the notochord ends at the level of the pituitary gland (center of the sella turcica).

• Prechordal chondrocranium (anterior) — from neural crest → ethmoid, lesser wing of sphenoid
• Chordal chondrocranium (posterior) — from paraxial mesoderm → greater wing of sphenoid, petrous temporal, basioccipital
Langman's Fig. 10.6 — Dorsal view of the chondrocranium. Prechordal (neural crest, blue) lies anterior to the sella turcica; chordal (paraxial mesoderm, red) lies posterior. From Langman's Medical Embryology, 15e.

Composite Bones: Sphenoid + Occipital + Temporal

These are "composite" because they receive contributions from both neural crest and paraxial mesoderm, forming via both intramembranous and endochondral ossification. Development begins at week 5, the parts fuse by about age 6, and the sphenoid and occipital meet at the sphenobasilar synchondrosis (SBS) — which doesn't fully fuse until approximately age 25 (high-yield for OMM).

Composite Bone Development — Development timeline from week 5 through week 9, showing trabeculated cartilage, polar cartilage, otic capsules, and progressive incorporation into sphenoid. Lecture notes.

Cranial Base: Step-by-Step (Weeks 5–9)

Chondrocranium Development — Left: early stage showing trabecula, hypophyseal cartilage, parachordal cartilage, otic capsules, and occipital somites around the notochord. Right: later stage with nasal septum, orbitosphenoid, and alisphenoid forming. Lecture notes.
Weeks 5–6

Trabeculated cartilage develops. Polar cartilage surrounds the hypophyseal stalk → will become part of the sphenoid. Notochord is surrounded by parachordal cartilage. Two otic capsules appear lateral to the parachordal cartilage, housing the acousticovestibular organ. Occipital somites begin forming the basicranium.

Weeks 7–8

Trabeculated cartilage → nasal capsule inside the ethmoid. Optic capsules enlarge. Polar cartilage is incorporated into the sphenoid. Orbitosphenoid (ala orbitalis) and alisphenoid (alar temporalis) develop. Ossification of cartilaginous structures begins forming the cranial base.

Weeks 8–9

Orbitosphenoid → lesser wing of sphenoid. Alisphenoid → greater wing of sphenoid. Maxillary (V2) and mandibular (V3) nerves pass through foramina in the greater wing via the middle cranial fossa.

Viscerocranium (Facial Skeleton)

Facial bones derive from neural crest cells through the first two pharyngeal arches:

1st pharyngeal arch — dorsal portion (maxillary process → maxilla, zygomatic, part of temporal) and ventral portion (mandibular process → Meckel cartilage; mandible ossifies intramembranously around it). The dorsal tip gives rise to the malleus and incus.

2nd pharyngeal arch → stapes. All three ossicles begin ossifying in the 4th month, making them the first bones in the body to fully ossify.

Sutures & Fontanelles

At birth, flat skull bones are separated by sutures (narrow connective tissue seams). Where 2+ bones meet, sutures widen into fontanelles. Their purposes: allow molding during birth and permit postnatal brain growth.

Sutures, Fontanelles & Dural Folds — Including the anterior transverse dural septum, tentorium cerebelli, and labeled newborn skull. Lecture notes.
Langman's Fig. 10.5 — Skull of a newborn from above (A) and the right side (B). Note the anterior and posterior fontanelles. The posterior fontanelle closes ~3 months after birth; the anterior closes around 18 months. From Langman's Medical Embryology, 15e.
SutureWhat it separates
LambdoidOccipital from parietal bones
SagittalThe two parietal bones (midline)
CoronalParietal bones from frontal bones
MetopicThe two frontal bones
FontanelleFormed byCloses
Anterior2 frontal + 2 parietal bones~18 months
Posterior2 parietal + 1 occipital bone~1–2 months
Clinical Pearl Palpation of the anterior fontanelle assesses ossification progress and intracranial pressure. Bulging → elevated ICP. Sunken → possible dehydration.

Craniosynostosis vs. Deformational Plagiocephaly

Craniosynostosis (Synostosis)

Premature fusion of one or more cranial sutures (~1 in 2,500 births). Primarily genetic. The skull cannot grow perpendicular to the fused suture and compensates by growing parallel to it.

TypeSutureSkull Shape
ScaphocephalySagittal (57%)Long & narrow — no lateral growth
BrachycephalyBoth coronalShort & tall — no AP growth
OxycephalyCoronalTower-shaped — no posterior growth
Plagiocephaly (synostotic)Unilateral coronalAsymmetric; trapezoidal from above
Key Genetic Associations FGFR1/FGFR2 → Pfeiffer, Apert, Crouzon, Jackson-Weiss syndromes
FGFR3 → Achondroplasia, thanatophoric dysplasia
TWIST1 → Saethre–Chotzen (coronal synostosis + polydactyly)
MSX2 → Boston-type craniosynostosis
RUNX2 → Cleidocranial dysplasia (delayed closure, absent clavicles)

Deformational Plagiocephaly

This is fundamentally different — it is an environmental / positional condition, not a suture fusion problem. It occurs when an infant lies in one position too long, causing asymmetric gravitational forces to restrict growth on one side.

Synostosis vs. Deformational Plagiocephaly — Left: scaphocephaly (sagittal) and oxycephaly (coronal). Right: deformational plagiocephaly showing ipsilateral flattening, ear displacement, and contralateral occipital bossing. Lecture notes.
Characteristic Findings • Ipsilateral occipitoparietal flattening (the side the baby lies on)
• Ipsilateral frontal bossing (compensatory anterior protrusion)
• Ipsilateral ear displaced anteriorly
• Contralateral occipital bossing
• Skull viewed from above → parallelogram shape
Key Differentiator In deformational plagiocephaly, all sutures are patent. The skull takes a parallelogram shape from above. In synostotic plagiocephaly, a suture is fused and the skull is trapezoidal from above.
Treatment — OMM Relevance Repositioning (tummy time, alternating head position) is first-line. Helmeting (cranial orthosis) redirects growth forces if caught early (<12 months). OMT — cranial osteopathic techniques can address fascial/membranous restrictions. Surgery is not indicated (no fused suture to release).

Other Clinical Correlates

Cranioschisis / Anencephaly Failure of the cranial neuropore to close → cranial vault never forms → brain tissue degenerates in amniotic fluid → anencephaly (incompatible with life). Smaller defects (cranial meningocele, meningoencephalocele) may be operable.
Achondroplasia Most common skeletal dysplasia (1/20,000). Gain-of-function FGFR3 mutation → abnormal endochondral ossification affecting long bones and cranial base. AD; 90% de novo. Features: megalocephaly, small midface, short limbs, spinal curvature.
Microcephaly Usually secondary — brain fails to grow, so skull doesn't expand. Causes: maternal PKU, IUGR, fetal alcohol exposure. Often with significant intellectual disability.
